TEXT.citation: Early, R. J., & Whitehorn, J. (2003). One hundred Paiwan texts. Pacific Linguistics, Research School of Pacific and Asian Studies, The Australian National University.
TEXT.BibTeX_citation: @book{100paiwantexts, author = {Robert Early and John Whitehorn}, title = {One Hundred Paiwan Texts}, year = {2003}, publisher = {Pacific Linguistics, Research School of Pacific and Asian Studies, The Australian National University}}
TEXT.source: told by Giɫgiɫaw of Vaɫulu village

S.FORM: aitsu a ngatsi maretimaɫimaɫi a papungadan.
S.PHON: aiʦu a ŋaʦi marətimaʎimaʎi a papuŋadan.
S.FORM: aicu a ngaci maretimaljimalji a papungadan.
S.PHON: aiʦu a ŋaʦi marətimaʎimaʎi a papuŋadan.
S.TRANSL: Snails are known by various names.

S.FORM: azua zuma nu qaɫan ḍuɫi aya; azua zuma nu qaɫan ngatsingatsi aya; azua zuma nu qaɫan tsirat aya.
S.PHON: azua zuma nu qaʎan ɖuʎi aja; azua zuma nu qaʎan ŋaʦiŋaʦi aja; azua zuma nu qaʎan ʦirat aja.
S.FORM: azua zuma nu qaljan drulji aya; azua zuma nu qaljan ngacingaci aya; azua zuma nu qaljan cirat aya.
S.PHON: azua zuma nu qaʎan ɖuʎi aja; azua zuma nu qaʎan ŋaʦiŋaʦi aja; azua zuma nu qaʎan ʦirat aja.
S.TRANSL: Some villages call them ḍuɫi, some ngatsingatsi, some tsirat.

S.FORM: aitsu a ngatsi selapay nu napesakuyakuya aravats nu tja patsunan.
S.PHON: aiʦu a ŋaʦi səɭapaj nu napəsakujakuja aravaʦ nu ca paʦunan.
S.FORM: aicu a ngaci selapay nu napesakuyakuya aravac nu tja pacunan.
S.PHON: aiʦu a ŋaʦi səɭapaj nu napəsakujakuja aravaʦ nu ʦa paʦunan.
S.TRANSL: Snails are extremely unpleasant to look at;

S.FORM: ɫakua sanguaq aravats nu tja kanen.
S.PHON: ʎakua saŋuaq aravaʦ nu ca kanən.
S.FORM: ljakua sanguaq aravac nu tja kanen.
S.PHON: ʎakua saŋuaq aravaʦ nu ʦa kanən.
S.TRANSL: but they are very tasty to eat.

S.FORM: a sisan kakanenan tja alapen azua seti sakamaya, sa tja senavi tua qavu a seman lialiaw, tua sika sualapan nazua djarayadjayadjay, sana tja qirqiri tua ɫawɫaw, sa tja tau'iyu-i, sa tja djamayan.
S.PHON: a sisan kakanənan ca aɭapən azua səti sakamaja, sa ca sənavi tua qavu a səman ɭiaɭiaw, tua sika suaɭapan nazua ɉarajaɉajaɉaj, sana ca qirqiri tua ʎawʎaw, sa ca tau'iju-i, sa ca ɉamajan.
S.FORM: a sisan kakanenan tja alapen azua seti sakamaya, sa tja senavi tua qavu a seman lialiaw, tua sika sualapan nazua djarayadjayadjay, sana tja qirqiri tua ljawljaw, sa tja tau'iyu-i, sa tja djamayan.
S.PHON: a sisan kakanənan ʦa aɭapən azua səti sakamaja, sa ʦa sənavi tua qavu a səman ɭiaɭiaw, tua sika suaɭapan nazua ɉarajaɉajaɉaj, sana ʦa qirqiri tua ʎawʎaw, sa ʦa tauʔiju-i, sa ʦa ɉamajan.
S.TRANSL: When we are going to prepare them to eat, we take the meat only and clean it many times over with ash/lime to get rid of the stickiness; them we fry them in oil, add soya sauce, and eat them as a side dish.

S.FORM: ɫakua inika amin a san djamayin; nanguaq uta a tsavuin tua ragrag tua sutjaw.
S.PHON: ʎakua inika amin a san ɉamajin; naŋuaq uta a ʦavuin tua ragrag tua sucaw.
S.FORM: ljakua inika amin a san djamayin; nanguaq uta a cavuin tua ragrag tua sutjaw.
S.PHON: ʎakua inika amin a san ɉamajin; naŋuaq uta a ʦavuin tua ragrag tua suʦaw.
S.TRANSL: But they are not only eaten as a side dish; they are also good wrapped up with pounded dry taro or peanuts.
We certainly lick our lips after eating them.

S.FORM: qau aitsu a ngatsi ika tjen a pakakeḍikeḍi nu malap itjen.
S.PHON: qau aiʦu a ŋaʦi ika cən a pakakəɖikəɖi nu maɭap icən.
S.FORM: qau aicu a ngaci ika tjen a pakakedrikedri nu malap itjen.
S.PHON: qau aiʦu a ŋaʦi ika ʦən a pakakəɖikəɖi nu maɭap iʦən.
S.TRANSL: We don’t just get a few snails.

S.FORM: mekeḍi aravats nu tja san kakanenen; saka nu ɫemliaw itjen sekuletj itjen.
S.PHON: məkəɖi aravaʦ nu ca san kakanənən; saka nu ʎəmɭiaw icən səkuɭəc icən.
S.FORM: mekedri aravac nu tja san kakanenen; saka nu ljemliaw itjen sekuletj itjen.
S.PHON: məkəɖi aravaʦ nu ʦa san kakanənən; saka nu ʎəmɭiaw iʦən səkuɭəʦ iʦən.
S.TRANSL: They go away to nothing when we cook them; so we get lots in order to have enough to eat.
